recommendation_rationale.description: A detailed justification for the final recommendation, explaining how the chosen library excels across the most critical criteria, such as TypeScript ergonomics, multi-edge support, serialization fidelity, and ecosystem maturity.

library_name.description: The name of the graph library being profiled.

api_ergonomics_and_typing.description: Analysis of API ergonomics with TypeScript, including the quality of type definitions and support for generics for node/edge attributes.

multi_edge_support.description: Details on the library's native support for modeling multiple, distinct edges between the same two nodes, each with its own attributes.

serialization_fidelity.description: Evaluation of the library's JSON serialization capabilities, including round-trip fidelity, preservation of attributes and multi-edges, and schema design.

algorithm_coverage.description: Overview of the graph theory algorithms available, such as topological sort, SCC, shortest paths, and clustering, whether in-core or via plugins.

performance_characteristics.description: Information on runtime performance, any available benchmarks, and suitability for large-scale graphs.

bundle_size.description: Metrics on the library's bundle size (e.g., ESM, minified + gzipped) and notes on tree-shaking effectiveness for a data-model-only use case.

maintenance_and_adoption.description: Assessment of maintenance velocity (2024-2026), community health, and notable production users or case studies.

ir_strategy_guidance.description: Guidance on whether the library's native types are suitable as an Intermediate Representation (IR) or if wrapping them is recommended.

overall_assessment.description: A summary of the library's key pros, cons, and overall fitness for the specified plugin requirements.

library_profiles.description: In-depth technical profiles for each of the primary evaluated graph libraries (Graphology, ngraph, Cytoscape.js). Each profile will detail API ergonomics with TypeScript generics, multi-edge support implementation, serialization fidelity and schema, available algorithms, performance benchmarks, bundle size metrics, maintenance velocity (2024-2026), and known production users.

typescript_ergonomics_comparison.description: A comparative analysis of the developer experience (DX) and API ergonomics when using TypeScript generics for typed node and edge attributes across the evaluated libraries. Includes examples of typing patterns and potential pitfalls.

multi_edge_support_comparison.description: A detailed comparison of how each library models and handles multiple edges between the same two nodes, including the ability to store distinct attributes for each parallel edge and the API for accessing them.

serialization_and_ir_strategy_comparison.description: An analysis of each library's JSON serialization capabilities, including round-trip guarantees, preservation of multi-edges and metadata, and strategies for schema evolution. Provides guidance on whether to use native types as the Intermediate Representation (IR) or wrap them in a stable schema.

algorithm_coverage_comparison.description: A comparative overview of the graph theory algorithms (e.g., topological sort, Strongly Connected Components (SCC), shortest path, clustering) available either in-core or through the ecosystem of each library.

bundle_size_summary.description: A comparative summary of bundle size metrics (ESM, min+gzip) and tree-shaking effectiveness across the evaluated libraries.

performance_summary.description: A comparative summary of runtime performance characteristics and any available benchmarks on representative graphs.

maintenance_velocity_summary.description: A comparative summary of maintenance activity, including commit frequency and release cadence from 2024-2026.

community_health_summary.description: A comparative assessment of community health indicators like the number of contributors, bus factor, and issue/PR activity.

production_adoption_summary.description: A comparative overview of known production users and significant case studies for each library.

live_reload_preview_architecture.description: A description of architectural patterns for implementing a live-reload browser preview. This covers the flow from the graph model, through JSON serialization, to a renderer like Mermaid or D2.

role_clarification.description: Clarification of elkjs's role as a dedicated layout engine, distinct from a graph modeling library.

api_and_configuration.description: Details on the TypeScript API, the richness of layout configuration options, and any build considerations (e.g., WASM, workers).

input_output_schema.description: Description of the ELK-JSON format it expects as input and returns as output, and how to adapt other graph models to it.

performance_notes.description: Analysis of layout performance on medium to large graphs and strategies for optimization.

integration_patterns.description: Architectural patterns for integrating elkjs into a live-reload preview pipeline with renderers like Mermaid or D2.

limitations_and_fallbacks.description: Known limitations of the library and recommended fallback strategies for very large or dynamic graphs.

library_name.description: The name of the library being analyzed.

typescript_typing_quality.description: Assessment of the library's TypeScript support, including quality of types and support for generics.

multi_edge_support.description: Analysis of the library's capability to model multiple edges between the same nodes.

serialization_and_algorithms.description: Notes on the library's serialization features and the breadth of its algorithm coverage.

performance_and_bundle_size.description: Summary of the library's performance characteristics and bundle size.

overall_fit_and_summary.description: A concluding summary of the library's pros, cons, and overall fit for the non-UI data model use case.

rising_alternatives_2024_2026.description: An overview of any new or rising TypeScript-native graph libraries identified during the research that show promise but may not yet have the maturity or feature-completeness of the primary candidates.

decision_matrix_summary.description: A concluding summary presented as a decision matrix or a structured comparison, weighing each library against the critical plugin requirements: Typed Attributes, Multi-Edge Support, Serialization, Algorithms, Performance, and Maintenance.
